title: STEMI and NSTEMI: Pathophysiology, ECG Changes and CTICU Management

abridged.title: Abridged Snapshot
abridged.content:
Bedside recognition of ACS in the CTICU patient

STEMI and NSTEMI are both acute coronary syndromes caused by abrupt reduction in coronary blood flow. In the cardiothoracic ICU the context is wider than an emergency department presentation: the patient may be post-CABG with graft failure, post-valve with coronary obstruction, post-aortic surgery with coronary malperfusion, or a medical cardiac ICU patient awaiting intervention. The bedside priority is to recognise myocardial ischaemia early, connect ECG changes to haemodynamic deterioration, and escalate before low cardiac output becomes established.

STEMI — ST-elevation myocardial infarction, usually caused by acute coronary artery occlusion producing transmural ischaemia and ST elevation in anatomically related ECG leads.

NSTEMI — Non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction, usually caused by subtotal coronary occlusion, plaque rupture with thrombus, supply-demand mismatch, or microvascular impairment producing troponin rise without persistent ST elevation.

Reciprocal change — ST depression in electrically opposite ECG leads. It strengthens the diagnosis of acute STEMI and can help localise the infarct territory.

Finding Bedside meaning Immediate action
New ST elevation in contiguous leads Acute transmural injury until proven otherwise. Repeat 12-lead, compare with baseline, check posterior/right-sided leads if indicated, notify medical team urgently and prepare for reperfusion pathway.
ST depression with chest pain, hypotension or new arrhythmia NSTEMI, posterior STEMI, left main/proximal LAD ischaemia, or supply-demand mismatch. Escalate urgently, obtain serial ECG/troponin, optimise oxygen delivery, treat hypotension/anaemia/arrhythmia, anticipate cath lab discussion.
New LBBB or paced rhythm with shock/ischaemic symptoms May obscure STEMI pattern. Use modified criteria if trained, compare old ECG, look for concordant ST changes, escalate rather than dismiss.
Post-CABG sudden hypotension, ventricular arrhythmia, rising lactate Graft problem, perioperative MI, tamponade, bleeding, or low cardiac output syndrome. Call senior help, check ECG/echo/labs/drains, prepare vasoactive support and possible urgent theatre/cath lab.

Escalate immediately for persistent chest pain, new ST elevation, haemodynamic collapse, sustained VT/VF, MAP <60 mmHg despite support, new severe LV dysfunction on echo, or suspected graft/coronary complication after cardiac surgery.

expanded.title: Expanded Deep-Dive
expanded.content:
Mechanism: why STEMI and NSTEMI behave differently

Myocardial oxygen balance depends on coronary blood flow, arterial oxygen content, haemoglobin, diastolic perfusion pressure, heart rate, ventricular wall tension and metabolic demand. A coronary plaque rupture with thrombosis may fully occlude the artery, producing STEMI, or partially occlude it, producing NSTEMI. In CTICU, ischaemia may also occur without plaque rupture: anaemia, hypotension, tachyarrhythmia, hypoxaemia, coronary spasm, graft failure, air embolism, poor cardioplegia protection, coronary ostial obstruction after valve/aortic root surgery, or severe LV hypertrophy can all create oxygen supply-demand mismatch.

ST elevation reflects an injury current from myocardium undergoing acute transmural ischaemia. It is usually measured at the J point. In practical bedside terms, ST elevation in two anatomically contiguous leads is more meaningful than an isolated lead change. Inferior STEMI appears in II, III and aVF; anterior STEMI in V1–V4; lateral STEMI in I, aVL, V5–V6; posterior STEMI may present as ST depression V1–V3 with tall R waves and requires posterior leads V7–V9.

NSTEMI is a clinical diagnosis requiring evidence of myocardial injury, usually troponin rise/fall, with compatible symptoms, ECG change, imaging evidence or angiographic findings. The CTICU nurse must not wait for troponin if the patient is unstable. Dynamic ST depression, T-wave inversion, new arrhythmia, unexplained hypotension, rising lactate, falling SvO2/ScvO2, pulmonary oedema or escalating inotrope need can indicate active ischaemia before biomarkers peak.

ECG territories and complications

Territory Typical leads High-risk complications
Inferior / RCA II, III, aVF; reciprocal I/aVL. Bradycardia, AV block, RV infarction, hypotension worsened by nitrates or hypovolaemia.
Anterior / LAD V1–V4, sometimes I/aVL. Large LV injury, cardiogenic shock, VT/VF, LV aneurysm, mural thrombus.
Lateral / circumflex/diagonal I, aVL, V5–V6. May be subtle; can cause LV dysfunction and mitral regurgitation if papillary muscle involved.
Posterior ST depression V1–V3; ST elevation V7–V9. Often missed; may be circumflex occlusion; treat as STEMI equivalent when criteria met.
Left main / proximal LAD pattern Diffuse ST depression with ST elevation aVR/V1. Severe global subendocardial ischaemia; high shock/arrest risk.

Post-cardiac surgery ACS pattern

After CABG, the classic complaint of crushing chest pain may be absent because the patient is sedated, ventilated, analgesed, delirious or neuropathic. Ischaemia may present as new ventricular ectopy, increasing pacing requirement, low cardiac output, rising lactate, new regional wall motion abnormality, difficulty weaning from bypass, pulmonary oedema, or sudden vasopressor/inotrope escalation. A graft occlusion may cause acute regional infarction. An internal mammary graft problem may produce anterior ischaemia; vein graft thrombosis can produce territory-specific STEMI/NSTEMI.

After aortic valve/root surgery, coronary ostial compromise is rare but catastrophic. A patient with sudden LV failure, ventricular arrhythmia, new ST elevation or inability to separate from bypass needs immediate surgical/cardiology review. After TAVI, coronary obstruction is uncommon but time-critical and often presents with abrupt hypotension, ST change and ventricular dysfunction.

pearls.title: Clinical Pearls
pearls.content:
Clinical Pearls

A post-op cardiac patient can infarct without telling you about chest pain. Treat unexplained new arrhythmia, low cardiac output, rising lactate, or new ST change as ischaemia until you have actively excluded it.

Inferior STEMI plus hypotension should make you think of RV infarction. These patients may be preload dependent, can deteriorate with nitrates, and often dislike tachycardia because RV filling and coronary perfusion are compromised.

Posterior infarction is commonly hidden. Horizontal ST depression in V1–V3 with tall R waves is not automatically 'anterior ischaemia'; request posterior leads V7–V9 if the clinical story fits.

Do not dismiss ST changes because the patient has pacing spikes, bundle branch block, post-operative inflammation or baseline abnormalities. Compare with previous ECGs and escalate if the patient is unstable.

After CABG, a new regional wall motion abnormality on echo plus rising lactate and escalating inotrope is often more clinically important than waiting for a troponin, because troponin is commonly elevated after cardiac surgery.

equipment.title: Equipment Tie-In
equipment.content:
Equipment Tie-In

12-lead ECG acquisition

Monitor ST trends

Bedside monitors may trend ST deviation, but monitor-derived ST alarms are only as good as lead placement, filtering and baseline selection. Use monitor trends as an early warning, not a definitive diagnosis. Confirm with diagnostic 12-lead ECG whenever possible.

Nurse-technician checks

Problem Effect Fix
Loose ECG electrode False ST shift, rhythm misclassification, missed arrhythmia. Replace electrodes, prep skin, reduce cable tension.
Wrong V lead position False infarct pattern or missed territory. Reposition using anatomical landmarks.
Pacing artefact ST segment hard to interpret. Print ECG with pacing visible; compare native beats if present; ask for expert review.
Electrical interference Noisy baseline obscures ST segment. Check warming devices, beds, pumps, cables and electrode quality.

targets.title: Targets and Numbers
targets.content:
Targets and Numbers

Parameter Target / threshold CT-ICU interpretation
ST elevation Classically ≥1 mm in two contiguous limb leads or ≥2 mm in contiguous precordial leads, with sex/age-specific V2–V3 thresholds used in formal criteria. In an unstable CTICU patient, dynamic contiguous ST elevation is an emergency even if exact criteria are difficult due to pacing or baseline abnormality.
Posterior leads V7–V9 ≥0.5 mm ST elevation is often significant; ≥1 mm has higher specificity. Use when V1–V3 ST depression suggests posterior STEMI.
Troponin Rise/fall above 99th percentile supports myocardial injury; post-cardiac surgery interpretation requires context. Do not use isolated post-op troponin elevation alone to diagnose graft failure; correlate with ECG, echo and haemodynamics.
Heart rate in ischaemia Avoid marked tachycardia; commonly aim 60–90/min if perfusion allows. Tachycardia shortens diastole and reduces coronary perfusion time.
Haemoglobin Often maintain ≥80 g/L in stable ICU; higher thresholds may be chosen in active ischaemia/shock depending on senior plan. Anaemia reduces oxygen delivery and may worsen supply-demand mismatch.
MAP Usually 65–80 mmHg after routine CABG/valve surgery; often 70–90 mmHg after complex aortic surgery or chronic hypertension if ordered. Treat the number in context: lactate, urine output, mentation/sedation, skin perfusion, SvO2/ScvO2, echo and surgical goals.
Urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr; commonly ≥30 mL/hr in average adult; rising concern if <0.3 mL/kg/hr for 2 hours. Low UO with hypotension and narrow pulse pressure suggests low flow until proven otherwise.
Lactate Usually expected to trend down; concern if >2 mmol/L and rising; high concern if ≥4 mmol/L or rising despite support. After CPB, lactate may be elevated, but persistent rise suggests oxygen debt, shock, bleeding, tamponade, severe vasoplegia or poor cardiac output.
Potassium Common rhythm target 4.0–4.5 mmol/L in CTICU unless renal failure/hyperkalaemia risk. Low K increases ectopy, AF, VT risk and digoxin sensitivity.
Magnesium Common rhythm target ≥0.8 mmol/L; many CTICUs target 1.0 mmol/L in AF/VT risk. Low Mg promotes ventricular ectopy and AF; replacement is common post bypass.

quiz.question: A ventilated post-CABG patient develops new ST elevation in II, III and aVF with hypotension. What is the safest interpretation?
options: Electrode artefact only; no action unless chest pain is reported
Expected after bypass and not urgent
quiz.explanation: Contiguous inferior ST elevation with hypotension after CABG may indicate acute coronary/graft complication or RV involvement. Sedated patients may not report chest pain. Escalation, repeat ECG and haemodynamic assessment are urgent.

quiz.question: Horizontal ST depression in V1–V3 with tall R waves should prompt consideration of which diagnosis?
quiz.explanation: Posterior MI can appear as reciprocal anterior ST depression in V1–V3 with tall R waves. Posterior leads V7–V9 should be considered.

quiz.question: After aortic root surgery, abrupt hypotension, ventricular arrhythmia and new LV dysfunction should trigger concern for:
options: Coronary ostial compromise or acute coronary malperfusion
quiz.explanation: Aortic root and valve procedures can rarely compromise coronary flow. Sudden shock, ventricular arrhythmia and LV dysfunction are time-critical and require urgent senior/surgical review.
